data.title: Mapfre SA reports 1H results
data.description:

data.title: UK Manufacturing PMI rises to 52.8 in July, hitting two-month high
data.description:
• Manufacturing PMI in the United Kingdom increased to 52.80 points in July from 52.50 points in June of 2026. 
  • Services PMI in the United Kingdom increased to 51.80 points in July from 48.80 points in June of 2026.
  • Composite PMI in the United Kingdom increased to 52.10 points in July from 49.30 points in June of 2026.

data.title: Eurozone flash PMIs hit multi-month highs in July as economic activity accelerates
data.description:
• Eurozone flash PMIs hit multi-month highs in July: the composite output index rose to 51.9, a 5-month high, from June's 50.0.
  • Services business activity reached 51.6 (5-month high; June: 49.4). Manufacturing PMI edged up to 52.0 (3-month high; June: 51.4).
  • The increase in output reflected both a renewed rise in services business activity and a faster expansion in manufacturing production.
  • In fact, the solid growth in manufacturing output seen in July was the sharpest since March 2022.
  • The euro area's largest economy – Germany –saw business activity increase for the first time in four months. Meanwhile, French output continued to fall, but only marginally.
  • The rest of the eurozone as a whole registered the strongest expansion in eight months.
  • The rise in output in July was in line with a renewed increase in new orders, the first in five months. Although modest, the rate of growth was the fastest since April 2023.

data.title: Stock index futures edge higher as investors seek rebound after Thursday selloff
data.description:
Stock index futures were higher on Friday as the stock market hoped to recover from Thursday's selloff. 

Dow futures (INDU) +0.33%, S&P 500 futures (SPX) +0.16%, and Nasdaq futures (US100:IND) +0.04%.

Wall Street's major averages slid on Thursday as tensions in the Middle East lifted oil prices above $100 per barrel, while investors digested the latest quarterly results from giants such as Tesla and Alphabet.

Investors will monitor the release of the S&P Global Composite Flash PMI and New Home Sales data later.

The 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) fell 0.5 basis points to 4.35%, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 0.2 basis points to 4.70%, and the 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) rose 1.2 basis points to 5.18%.

Among S&P 500 stocks, Intel (INTC) +4.37%, Edwards Lifesciences (EW) +3.94%, Digital Realty Trust (DLR) +2.46%, Oracle (ORCL) +2.05%, and Applied Materials (AMAT) +1.99%.

Deckers Outdoor (DECK) -3.59%, Viatris (VTRS) -2.95%, Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) -2.12%, Fox (FOXA) -1.98%, and NRG Energy (NRG) -1.94%.

data.title: Tesla's earnings slump leaves short sellers with over $9B in paper gains this year
data.description:
Tesla's (TSLA) steep selloff following its second-quarter earnings report delivered a windfall for investors betting against the electric vehicle maker.

Shares plunged 14.5% on Thursday, marking their biggest one-day decline in more than a year, after the company reported weaker-than-expected quarterly profit, renewing concerns about the pace of returns from its artificial intelligence and robotics investments.

The drop put Tesla's short sellers on track to reap roughly $4.3B in mark-to-market gains in a single day, Bloomberg reported, citing S3 Partners Managing Director Ihor Dusaniwsky.

Tesla (TSLA) remains the most heavily shorted stock among the Magnificent Seven, with roughly 3% of its shares sold short, according to S3 Partners. By comparison, the group's second-most shorted stock, Meta Platforms (META), has only 1.6% of its float held in short positions.

The stock also remains the priciest in the Magnificent Seven, trading at about 152 times forward earnings, while ranking as the group's worst performer this year. Following Thursday's slide, Tesla shares are down nearly 29% year to date, leaving short sellers sitting on approximately $9.08B in paper gains in 2026.

data.title: France services expected to improve in July
data.description:
• France's S&P Global Flash Composite PMI rose to 49.6 in July 2026 from 47.2 in the previous month, signaling the softest contraction since the Middle East conflict began in February. 
  • The services sector improved, with activity rising to a seven-month high of 49.8 in July from 46.8 in June, helped by the first increase in demand since November.
  • Manufacturing activity slipped to 48.8 from 49.1 as demand weakened.

data.title: Sallie Mae narrows 2026 net charge-off guidance to $365M-$385M in response to recovery strategy shift
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SLM Corporation (SLM) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "I'm pleased to announce that we have successfully delivered all of the additional products, features and functions we planned for this peak season, including enhancements to our medical, dental, law and MBA products and the launch of our new parent loan." (CEO & Director Jonathan Witter)
  • "While peak season is just beginning and it's too early for definitive conclusions, the application and volume trends for these new products... are at the higher end of our expectations or better." (CEO & Director Witter)
  • "GAAP diluted EPS in the second quarter was $0.29 per share." (CEO & Director Witter)
  • "Loan originations were $716 million, up nearly 4.5% from the prior year quarter." (CEO & Director Witter)
  • "We have observed activity affecting a small segment of borrowers... progressing directly through delinquency to default." (CEO & Director Witter)
  • "While these actions create an in-year headroom to potential recoveries previously estimated at approximately $25 million in 2026, we view the impact as largely a timing dynamic and expect our internal efforts to equal or exceed this recovery level over time." (CEO & Director Witter)
  • "For the second quarter of 2026, we generated $333 million of net interest income and $45 million of other income." (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Peter Graham)
  • "As you may remember... we completed the ASR during the second quarter, repurchasing a total of 9.3 million shares." (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham)

Outlook

  • "At this time, we are narrowing our net charge-off guidance range by maintaining the high end at $385 million and raising the low end to $365 million." (CEO & Director Witter)
  • "We are making this change in response to our adjusted recovery practices... While we continue to expect approximately a $25 million potential impact to recoveries in 2026... We are affirming all other guidance metrics." (CEO & Director Witter)
  • "Looking towards the second half of this year, we expect margin expansion to resume as excess liquidity is deployed into new loan originations during our peak season." (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham)

Financial Results

  • "Net interest income decreased by $44 million, while other income increased by $16 million, driven by growth in recurring program management fees from our strategic partnership and growth in servicing fee revenue." (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham)
  • "Net interest margin was 4.75% for the quarter... we believe the second quarter will likely represent the low point for margin this year." (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham)
  • "Net charge-offs for the quarter were $113 million, up from $94 million in the prior year quarter." (CEO & Director Witter)
  • "Private education loans delinquent 30 days or more were 3.7% of loans in repayment... and a decrease from 4% at the end of the first quarter of 2026." (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham)
  • "Our provision for credit losses was $126 million in the second quarter, down from $149 million in the year ago quarter." (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham)
  • "Noninterest expenses were $195 million, up $28 million from the year ago quarter." (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham)
  • "We ended the quarter with liquidity of 18.6% of total assets... total risk-based capital was 13.1% and common equity Tier 1 capital was 11.8%." (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham)

Q&A

  • Mark DeVries, Deutsche Bank AG, Research Division: "any color you can give us on kind of the trajectory" for NIM in the back half? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "we'll start to normalize probably closer to our long-term target range of kind of 5%."
  • Mark DeVries, Deutsche Bank AG, Research Division: updates on the new loan sale partner and TAM/credit box? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "I expect that... likely close in the third quarter or early fourth quarter... creating some opportunity for credit box expansion."
  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen, Research Division: detail on the KKR partnership economics? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "The existing partnership with KKR is going really well, according to plan... largely in line with the economics that we have in the first partnership."
  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen, Research Division: what is improving in credit outside the impacted segment? CEO & Director Witter: "we are seeing better than 75% success rate after 3 and 6 months... That is higher than our expectations" and "we are seeing general strength in the portfolio across the rest of our segments."
  • Sanjay Sakhrani, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: why loan yields down and how to get back toward 5% NIM? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "second quarter is kind of our lowest origination quarter" and "as we get into the heart of our peak season... yield patterns will sort of start to reemerge."
  • Sanjay Sakhrani, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: higher gain-on-sale this quarter? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "the price that we're getting upfront is both the initial disbursement as well as the gain on the second disbursement" and "that kind of 2%-ish is a good target."
  • Terry Ma, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: "talk about what's kind of contemplated in the back half EPS guide"? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "we still feel there's a viable path for us to get up into the range that we had previously raised to last quarter."
  • Terry Ma, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: seasonality for delinquencies in the back half? CEO & Director Witter: "I think the general seasonal patterns are probably right" while noting "a modest impact" from selling new originations.
  • Donald Fandetti, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: why pause recovery sales and could it return? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "halt all of our debt sales and pull, for a time period, all recoveries in-house" and "we certainly have an ability to change our strategies and turn that back on."
  • Donald Fandetti, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: seasoned loan sales and balance sheet expectations? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "we likely would do modestly more loan sales this year" and "sized that at sort of $1 billion-ish more than what we otherwise would."
  • Jeffrey Adelson, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: are sales affecting yields and how to think about yield recovery vs product mix? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "no adverse selection" and "carrying the extra liquidity is the real thing."
  • Jeffrey Adelson, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: path for efficiency ratio and role of partnerships? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "we've had a growth in fee-based revenue in excess of 50% year-over-year" and "The second partnership will just add additional scale to that."
  • Yuna Sohn, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: could Grad mix change medium-term NIM target? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "we have not updated our point of view... low to mid-5% range for NIM."
  • Yuna Sohn, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: employment/repayment trends for the 2026 graduating cohort? Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham: "it's too early."
  • Caroline Latta, BofA Securities, Research Division: competitive dynamics into peak and which products exceed expectations? CEO & Director Witter: "peak is really just a couple of days old" and "we are encouraged by those activity levels... at or slightly above our expectations."

Sentiment Analysis

  • "credit performance" and "debt resolution" drove a slightly negative-to-pressing analyst tone, including "anything that kind of approaches credit kind of gets people a little more antsy" (Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen, Research Division) and "why the pause on all recovery sales" (Donald Fandetti, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, including "we remain optimistic about our credit performance" (CEO & Director Jonathan Witter) and "it's a timing issue in large part" (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Graham).
  • Versus last quarter, Q2 carried more sensitivity around recoveries and post-default behavior, while Q1 emphasized capital actions and preparation for federal reforms.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "Diluted EPS in the first quarter was $1.54 per share" (CEO & Director Jonathan Witter, Q1) compared with "GAAP diluted EPS in the second quarter was $0.29 per share" (CEO & Director Witter, Q2), alongside management emphasis in Q2 that "second quarter will likely represent the low point for margin this year" (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Peter Graham, Q2).
  • Q1 focused on accelerated capital return and large loan sales, including "we executed a $2 billion seasoned loan portfolio sale during the quarter" (CEO & Director Witter, Q1), while Q2 centered on peak-season product rollout and early demand signals.
  • Guidance language shifted from Q1: "We expect our diluted earnings per common share for 2026 to be between $3.10 and $3.20" (CEO & Director Witter, Q1) to Q2: "narrowing our net charge-off guidance range" and "affirming all other guidance metrics" (CEO & Director Witter, Q2).
  • Analyst focus moved from partnership buildout and capital-light implications (Q1) toward NIM trajectory, debt resolution-related recoveries, and the timing of margin normalization (Q2).

Risks and Concerns

  • "many of these borrowers are engaging with debt resolution providers" and the company took steps "to increase control over our post-default recoveries" with an estimated "approximately $25 million" in-year recovery headroom in 2026. (CEO & Director Jonathan Witter)
  • "we made a decision... to sort of halt all of our debt sales and pull, for a time period, all recoveries in-house" with an expectation that internal strategies "yield on balance, a higher return." (Co-President, CFO & Treasurer Peter Graham)
  • "some modest pressure on sort of marketing expense" was noted heading into peak season. (CEO & Director Witter)

Final Takeaway

Management described early peak-season application and volume indicators for new Grad and parent products as tracking "at the higher end of our expectations or better," while also highlighting a discrete credit issue tied to third-party debt resolution activity that prompted a shift to in-house post-default recoveries. The company narrowed 2026 net charge-off guidance to $365 million-$385 million, reiterated expectations for second-half net interest margin expansion as liquidity is deployed into originations, and emphasized continued capital return capacity with $242 million remaining under its repurchase authorization and plans to substantially deploy it in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Germany's composite PMI expected to grow in July; manufacturing leads
data.description:
• The S&P Global Flash Germany Composite PMI Output Index climbed to 51.2 in July, up from 49.5 in June and registering above the 50.0 no-change mark for the first time in four months.
  • Germany's services activity rose to a four-month high of 49.6 in July from 48.6 in June. Manufacturing activity improved to a four-month high of 52.2 from 50.3 in June.
  • "The German economy made a positive start to the third quarter, with the Composite PMI returning to growth territory after having signalled a three-month spell of contraction following the outbreak of war in the Middle East. However, given the escalating hostilities in the region in the past week or so, which have put renewed upward pressure on global energy prices, the path to a sustainable recovery still seems very much uncertain." said Phil Smith, Economics Associate Director at S&P Global Market Intelligence.
